blocks.statement: I can write F = G·m₁·m₂/r², explain what each symbol means, and reason about how F changes when masses or distance change.

blocks.whatWeKnow: The asteroid feels gravity from the Sun and (weakly) Earth.
blocks.connection: THIS is the equation we've been building toward. The 1/r² geometry decides how steeply gravity grows as 2026-XJ approaches.

blocks.markdown:
## The equation that runs the solar system

**F = G · m₁ · m₂ / r²**

- **F** — the gravitational force between the two masses (newtons)
- **G** = 6.67 × 10⁻¹¹ N·m²/kg² — always the same. That's why we call it *universal*.
- **m₁** — mass of one object (kg)
- **m₂** — mass of the other object (kg)
- **r** — distance between the **centers** of the two objects (meters)
- **r²** — r SQUARED. Doubling r makes F drop to **1/4**. Tripling r makes F drop to **1/9**.

Every mass pulls on every other mass. Earth pulls the Moon; the Moon pulls Earth back (that's the tides). You pull on your neighbor — tiny, but real.

terms.definition: Every mass pulls on every other mass with a force F = G·m₁·m₂/r². Earth pulls the Moon; the Moon pulls Earth back (tides).
terms.cognate: Sp. gravitación universal · Pt. gravitação universal

blocks.title: r is between CENTERS
blocks.markdown: r is measured center-to-center, **not** surface-to-surface. For an Earth–asteroid calculation, r ≈ the gap between them **plus Earth's radius**.

blocks.title: Gravity is not just 'Earth pulling things down'
blocks.markdown: Every **pair** of masses attracts. The reason you don't feel your neighbor's pull is the sizes involved: with human-scale masses, G·m₁·m₂ is microscopic. It takes a planet-sized m to make F noticeable.

blocks.prompt: Explore F = G·m₁·m₂/r². Start with reasonable asteroid numbers, then test: what happens to F when you double r? Triple it? Double one of the masses? Write what you find.
If 2026-XJ doubles its distance from Earth, what happens to F_gravity? What if it triples its distance?
blocks.frame: At 2r, F drops to ___ of its old value. At 3r, F drops to ___. The pattern is called an ___-square law.

blocks.title: Read & practice
blocks.markdown: Open the reader and read **13.1–13.4** (the falling apple, the falling moon, the falling Earth, Newton's law of universal gravitation). Answer the questions beside the reading as you go.
